Sarah lives in Portland now. She's a marine biologist, which surprises no one who knew her as a child. She studies whale songs, trying to decode their meanings. "They're telling stories," she insists. "Passing down histories." I believe her. The sea remembers everything.

Sometimes I think about leaving, moving somewhere landlocked where the horizon is broken by mountains or trees, where the weather doesn't smell like storms three days before they arrive. But then evening comes, and the sun sets fire to the water, and the dolphins appear in the distance, their backs dark and shining, and I know I'm exactly where I belong.

The young couple who bought Tom's cottage asked me about him the other day. What was he like? Was he happy? I told them about the poetry and the chess games, about how he'd lower baskets of food to stranded seals, about the morning I found him teaching a group of children to tie sailor's knots with old rope. But I didn't tell them about his theory that lighthouses exist in multiple dimensions, or about the logbook where he recorded not just weather and ships, but dreams and conversations with the dead.

They wouldn't understand. Not yet. Give them a few winters here, a few storms that shake the windows and rattle the soul. Let them see the way the light bends strangely on certain foggy nights, how the beam seems to reach not just across the water but through time itself. Then they'll be ready for Tom's stories.

The sea remembers everything, but it shares its memories slowly, carefully, only with those who have the patience to listen. I'm still learning that patience, still discovering what it means to be remembered by something so vast and indifferent and beautiful.

Tonight, I'll walk down to the beach again. I'll look for more sea glass, but really I'll be looking for myself—in the waves, in the wind, in the endless conversation between land and water. And maybe, if I'm lucky, I'll understand a little more of what Tom was trying to tell me, all those years ago, about the sea's long memory and our small place within it.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The morning light filters through my window like honey through cheesecloth, golden and slow. I've been thinking about time lately—how it pools in some moments and rushes through others like water through cupped hands. Yesterday feels both immediate and ancient, a paradox I can't quite resolve.

My grandmother used to say that memories were like pressed flowers: flattened versions of something once dimensional and alive. I disagreed with her then, but now I understand. The weight of remembering changes the shape of what was.

There's a particular quality to 3 AM thoughts that daylight dissolves. In darkness, the mind becomes both architect and demolition crew, building elaborate kingdoms of worry only to tear them down with the same fervor. I've learned to keep a notebook by my bed, not to capture these thoughts but to release them—ink as exorcism.

The coffee shop on Third Street closed last month. I walked past yesterday and saw they'd already gutted it, wires hanging from the ceiling like exposed nerves. For five years, I wrote there every Tuesday morning, same table, same barista who knew to start my order when she saw me crossing the street. We construct these small rituals without thinking, and then marvel at the emptiness when they're gone.

I've been reading about octopuses lately. They have three hearts and blue blood, can change color in milliseconds, and possess a disturbing intelligence. Each arm can taste what it touches. Imagine that—your fingertips as tongues, the world a feast of textures. We think we're the pinnacle of evolution, but we can't even regenerate a lost limb.

My neighbor practices violin at odd hours. Not badly, but not well either. The same passage repeated, stumbling over the same transition. There's something both maddening and oddly comforting about it—the audible evidence of someone trying, failing, trying again. We're all amateur musicians in our own ways, practicing scales we'll never quite master.

The best conversation I ever had was mostly silence. We sat on a dock watching the sunset paint the lake orange and pink, and everything important passed between us without words. Language is a clumsy tool sometimes, like trying to perform surgery with mittens on.

I keep a running list of things I want to remember: the way rain sounds on different surfaces (tin roofs like applause, leaves like whispers), the specific blue of October skies, how my mother's handwriting changed as she aged, becoming looser, more forgiving. But the act of listing changes the remembering, pins it down when it wants to flutter.

There's a tree outside my building that's dying from the inside out. Still green on the outside, still producing leaves each spring, but I've seen inside where the trunk split—hollow, dark, probably housing generations of insects. It makes me think about facades, about the versions of ourselves we present while quietly hollowing.

Sometimes I walk through the city at dawn, before the machinery of daily life kicks into gear. The streets belong to joggers and insomniacs then, to delivery trucks and stray cats. There's an honesty to cities at that hour, like seeing someone without makeup—all the cracks and imperfections visible, but somehow more beautiful for it.

I've been trying to learn to bake bread. There's something primitive and satisfying about it—flour, water, salt, yeast, and time. Mostly time. You can't rush fermentation, can't negotiate with gluten development. In an age of instant everything, bread demands patience. My first dozen attempts were dense as bricks, but yesterday I finally achieved something approaching lightness. Small victories.

The English language has no word for the feeling of missing something you've never had. Other languages do—Portuguese has "saudade," Welsh has "hiraeth." We make do with approximations, dancing around the edges of emotions we can't name. Maybe that's why we write, paint, sing—to fill the gaps between words.

My phone died last week, and I waited three days before replacing it. Those three days felt like swimming upstream at first, then like floating. Without the constant ping of notifications, time stretched differently. I noticed things: the pattern frost makes on windows, how pigeons actually walk (heads bobbing like they're keeping time to unheard music), the particular way late afternoon light makes everything look nostalgic.

There's a certain age where you realize your parents are just people—flawed, trying, making it up as they go. Then another age where you realize you've become the same. The cycle continues, each generation thinking they'll do better, each discovering the same fundamental impossibilities.

I dream sometimes of libraries—endless rows of books I'll never have time to read. Is there anything sadder than the books we'll never open, the lives we'll never live, the conversations we'll never have? Or maybe there's freedom in that limitation, in being forced to choose, to curate our brief existence.

The city is planting new trees along my street. Saplings now, thin as wrists, staked against wind. In fifty years, they'll shade windows I'll never see, shelter people not yet born. There's faith in planting trees—a belief in tomorrow that transcends the planter.

We're all just making it up, aren't we? Pretending we know where we're going, why we're here, what it means. But sometimes, in the quiet moments between heartbeats, between breaths, something crystallizes. Not understanding exactly, but acceptance. The peaceful recognition that not knowing is its own kind of knowledge.

The light has shifted while I've been writing. Morning has become afternoon, honey become amber. Time pools and rushes. Words accumulate like sediment. And somewhere, in the space between what's said and unsaid, meaning shimmers—visible for just a moment before dissolving back into the ordinary mystery of being alive.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then, standing in his cluttered kitchen with its walls covered in yellowing nautical charts and the smell of pipe tobacco thick in the air. But now, walking along this same stretch of coast thirty years later, I think I understand what he meant.

The waves roll in with the same rhythm they always have, each one carrying stories from distant shores. There's something eternal about this motion, something that makes our human concerns seem both vitally important and cosmically insignificant at the same time. It's humbling and oddly comforting.

I've been thinking a lot about permanence lately. Maybe it's turning fifty, or maybe it's watching my daughter pack for college, folding her life into boxes with the casual efficiency of someone who doesn't yet understand that childhood rooms become museums to who we used to be. She's eager to leave, to start her real life, as she calls it. As if these eighteen years were just rehearsal.

The lighthouse is automated now. Has been for decades. The keeper's cottage where I used to drink tea and listen to stories about ghost ships and perfect storms stands empty, windows boarded up against the weather. Progress, they called it. Efficiency. But something was lost in the translation from human to machine—that daily act of climbing the spiral stairs, trimming the wick, polishing the lens. The ritual of it.

We're very good at replacing rituals with conveniences. Coffee pods instead of the meditative morning grind. GPS instead of the art of getting lost and finding yourself somewhere unexpected. Text messages instead of letters that took days to arrive and were all the more precious for it. I'm not naive enough to wish these advances away, but I wonder sometimes if we've confused ease with improvement.

The beach is emptier than it used to be. This stretch was never popular with tourists—too rocky, too far from town, no amenities to speak of. Just rocks and sand and the endless conversation between land and sea. That's why I've always loved it. You can walk for miles here and meet only sandpipers and the occasional seal hauled out on the rocks, watching you with mild curiosity.

I found a piece of sea glass yesterday, worn smooth by who knows how many years of tumbling in the surf. Green, probably from an old Coke bottle, transformed by time and pressure into something beautiful. My daughter would have loved it when she was younger, would have added it to the collection on her windowsill. Now it sits in my pocket, a small weight reminding me of all the treasures we stop looking for when we grow up.

The old keeper—Harold was his name—used to say that lighthouses were love letters written in light, messages to sailors saying "here is danger, but also here is home." He never married, claimed the lighthouse was wife enough for him, but I think he understood love better than most. The patient, steady kind that shows up every night without fail, that asks for nothing but gives everything.

I've been married twenty-three years next month. Some days it feels like yesterday, some days like a geological epoch. We've weathered our storms, my wife and I, though they were the quiet kind that don't make for dramatic retellings. The slow erosions of disappointment, the gradual accumulation of small kindnesses that eventually outweigh the hurts. Marriage, I've learned, is its own kind of lighthouse keeping—a daily commitment to showing up, to tending the light even when the seas are calm and it seems like no one needs what you're offering.

The sun is setting now, painting the sky in shades of orange and pink that no photograph ever quite captures. This is the time of day Harold loved best, the changing of the guard between day and night. He'd stand on the gallery around the lighthouse lens, one hand on the railing, watching the world transform. "Every sunset is different," he'd say, "but they're all saying the same thing—here's your chance to rest, to let go of what the day brought. Tomorrow you get to try again."

I should head back soon. My wife will be wondering where I've wandered off to, though she knows my habits well enough by now. These evening walks have become my own ritual, my way of making sense of the day. Sometimes I bring back sea glass or interesting shells. Sometimes just sand in my shoes and the smell of salt on my clothes.

The lighthouse beam is already sweeping across the darkening water, its automated precision lacking the subtle variations that came with Harold's hand on the controls. But it's still beautiful, still necessary. Ships still need warning. Sailors still need to know where home is.

Maybe that's what the sea remembers—not the specific waves or particular storms, but the constant truth of arrival and departure, the eternal rhythm of coming and going. We're all just passing through, really, temporary keepers of our particular stretch of coast. The best we can do is tend our lights faithfully, love who we can, and trust that something of what we offered will remain, worn smooth by time into its own kind of beauty.

The stars are coming out now, and I turn toward home, carrying the sea's memories in my pocket.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't understand what he meant until I inherited his post, along with his weather-beaten journal and a brass key that didn't seem to fit any lock in the tower.

My first night alone, I climbed the spiral stairs to light the beacon. The mechanism was ancient but reliable—a dance of gears and oil and prismatic glass that had guided ships for over a century. As the beam swept across the darkening waters, I noticed something odd. Where the light touched the waves, they seemed to shimmer differently, as if revealing patterns beneath the surface.

I found myself thinking about memory—how we carry our past like the ocean carries salt. Every experience dissolves into us, changing our composition in ways we don't always recognize. The keeper's journal was full of such observations, mixed with weather reports and maintenance logs. "Tuesday: Fixed the fog horn. Southwest wind. The water spoke of the Annabelle again."

The Annabelle, I learned, was a merchant vessel that had foundered on the rocks below in 1892. Thirty-seven souls lost. But according to the keeper's increasingly erratic entries, on certain nights when the conditions were right—new moon, spring tide, wind from the east—the lighthouse beam would reveal the ship still sailing, trying to find safe harbor.

I dismissed it as an old man's fancy until my fourteenth night.

The beam swept east, and there it was: a three-masted ship where no ship should be, sailing against the wind. My rational mind cycled through explanations—a trick of fog, a distant vessel misperceived, phosphorescent algae. But as I watched the light pass over it again and again, I knew I was seeing something that existed in the margin between memory and reality.

The brass key grew warm in my pocket.

I descended to the lighthouse's base, to a door I'd never noticed before—or perhaps it had never been there before. The key fit perfectly. Inside was a room filled with bottles, hundreds of them, each containing what looked like seawater. Labels in the old keeper's handwriting: "Storm of '03," "Catherine's tears," "The day the whales came," "Forgiveness."

One bottle was empty except for a label: "For the next keeper."

Understanding came like the tide—inevitable and transforming. The lighthouse didn't just guide ships through physical space. It held the sea's memories, keeping watch over moments that were too important to let dissolve completely into the past. And the keeper's role was to tend these memories, to ensure that what needed remembering wasn't lost to the depths.

I walked back outside. The Annabelle was still there, caught in its eternal attempt to reach safety. I uncorked the empty bottle and held it to the wind. Slowly, something filled it—not water, but something that looked like liquid starlight. When I sealed it and returned inside, the ship was gone.

But I knew it would return. They all would—every memory the sea had swallowed, every story that needed witnessing. And I would be here, keeping the light burning, maintaining the delicate machinery that bridges what was and what is.

The next morning, I found a new entry in the keeper's journal, in my own handwriting: "Monday: Cleaned the lens. North wind. The sea remembers everything, but someone must help it speak."

Some jobs, I've learned, choose you. They wait, patient as tides, until you're ready to understand what they really ask of you. The lighthouse stands on the edge of two worlds, and I am its translator, turning the sea's vast memory into something human hearts can hold.

When my replacement comes—and they will come, drawn by some instinct they won't quite understand—I'll leave them the journal and the key. I'll tell them about the lights on the water and the room full of bottled moments. Whether they believe me won't matter. 

The sea remembers everything. 

And now, so do I.

The beam sweeps on, faithful and eternal, and in its light, past and present dance together on the waves. Ships find their way home. Stories find their keepers. And somewhere between the machinery of the lighthouse and the mystery of the ocean, memory itself finds a way to live forever.

Tomorrow I'll oil the gears and polish the lens. I'll check the fog horn and record the weather. But tonight, I simply watch the light turn its ancient circle, knowing that I am part of something larger than myself—a long line of keepers stretching back through time, each of us holding our watch, tending the memories that must not be forgotten.

The brass key is warm in my pocket. The bottles sing softly in their secret room.

And the sea remembers everything.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman's hands trembled as she held the photograph, its edges worn soft from years of handling. In it, a young couple stood before a cherry tree in full bloom, their faces bright with the kind of hope that only exists before life has had its way with you. She couldn't remember the last time she'd looked at it without crying.

"Gran?" Her granddaughter's voice drifted from the kitchen. "Tea's ready."

She tucked the photo back into her cardigan pocket and shuffled toward the warmth of voices and cinnamon. The kitchen hummed with life—her daughter chopping vegetables, grandchildren arguing over a board game, the dog sprawled across the floor like a breathing rug. This was what remained. This was what mattered.

But still, the weight of the photograph pulled at her pocket like a stone.

Fifty-three years. That's how long she'd been carrying other people's secrets. First as a nurse, then as a mother, now as the keeper of family histories that younger generations were too busy to remember. She knew which uncle had gambling debts, which cousin's marriage was held together by habit alone, which nephew still cried about the father who'd left when he was seven.

"Tell us about Grandpa," the youngest grandchild said suddenly, as if reading her thoughts.

The kitchen stilled. Even the dog lifted his head.

"What would you like to know?" she asked, settling into her chair with the careful movements of someone whose bones remembered every fall.

"Everything."

She smiled at that. Everything. As if a life could be condensed into an afternoon's telling. As if love and loss and the thousand small betrayals and forgivenesses that make a marriage could be served alongside tea and biscuits.

"Your grandfather," she began, "was terrible at dancing."

They laughed, but she was already somewhere else. A church hall, 1962. Paper streamers and borrowed shoes. Him stepping on her feet, apologizing with every turn, his hands shaking worse than hers did now. She'd known then that he was the one—not because of butterflies or fireworks, but because when he stepped on her feet for the fifth time, she'd thrown back her head and laughed, and he'd laughed too, and suddenly the whole room had seemed brighter.

"But he learned," she continued. "Every Tuesday for six months, he took lessons at the community center while I was at work. Never told me. Then at our wedding..." She paused, lost in the memory of surprise, his confident hand on her waist, the way he'd guided her across the floor like he'd been doing it all his life.

"He was full of surprises?" her daughter prompted gently.

"Yes." The word came out heavier than intended.

There were other surprises. The diagnosis at forty-five. The way strong hands could become fragile. The promises made in hospital rooms at 3 AM. The way grief could start before someone was even gone, a shadow growing longer each day.

But also: the surprise of daffodils planted secretly in autumn, blooming outside her window the spring after he died. The surprise of finding his handwriting in library books—notes in margins meant only for her. The surprise of still feeling married, even now, even after eight years of empty chairs and cold beds.

"He sounds wonderful," the eldest grandchild said.

"He was," she agreed. "And he wasn't. He was human."

This was the gift she wanted to give them, though she doubted they were ready to receive it. The understanding that love wasn't perfection. It was choosing to stay through the imperfections. It was forgiveness served daily, like bread. It was learning that sometimes the most romantic thing you could do was hold someone's hair back when they were sick, or pretend not to notice when they cried at commercials, or make them laugh when their father's Alzheimer's had stolen another piece of their childhood.

The photograph in her pocket seemed to pulse with warmth. Tomorrow, she decided. Tomorrow she would show them. Not just the cherry tree couple with their untested hopes, but the other photos too. The ones from after—tired faces over birthday cakes, forced smiles at holidays when money was tight, genuine joy at small victories nobody else would understand. The whole story, not just the pretty parts.

"Gran's getting philosophical," someone said, not unkindly.

She smiled. Let them think what they wanted. Someday they'd understand that philosophy was just another word for survival, for finding meaning in the mess. Someday they'd have their own photographs worn soft at the edges, their own secrets to carry, their own stories to portion out in kitchens full of love and noise.

The dog shifted, pressing against her feet. Outside, snow began to fall, fat flakes catching the last light of day. Her daughter brought her tea, made exactly how she liked it—too strong, too sweet, perfect. The grandchildren returned to their game, voices rising with competition and joy.

This was what remained. This was what mattered.

And in her pocket, the photograph waited, patient as memory, ready when she was to tell the rest of the story.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe her then – I was twelve and certain that adults spoke in riddles just to confuse us. But now, standing at the edge of the same cliff thirty years later, I think I understand what she meant.

The waves below crash with the same rhythm they always have, yet each one carries something different. Today they bring the smell of rain from storms still brewing miles offshore. Tomorrow they might taste of salt from waters that touched distant shores I'll never see. The sea is both constant and ever-changing, like memory itself.

I've been thinking about permanence lately. About what lasts and what doesn't. My daughter asked me last week why we take photographs when we have our memories, and I found myself struggling to explain that memories fade, shift, become stories we tell ourselves. The photograph of my grandmother in my wallet shows a woman I barely recognize anymore – not because the image has deteriorated, but because my memory of her has evolved, layered with years of missing her.

There's a particular quality to morning light that makes everything seem possible. It's not quite hope – hope implies uncertainty. It's more like potential, the way a blank canvas holds every painting that could ever be. I wake early now, not because I have to, but because I don't want to miss that brief window when the world feels malleable.

Yesterday, I found a note I'd written to myself five years ago. "Remember this feeling," it said, but gave no other context. I have no idea what feeling I meant to preserve. Was it joy? Sorrow? That peculiar mix of both that comes with watching your children grow up? The note is useless now, a cipher without a key, but somehow that makes it more precious. It's proof that I once felt something worth remembering, even if the specifics have dissolved.

My neighbor grows tomatoes in her backyard. She's eighty-three and moves like water – slow but unstoppable. She gave me one yesterday, still warm from the sun, and when I bit into it, I was suddenly seven years old in my grandfather's garden. Isn't it strange how taste can collapse time? How a simple tomato can be a time machine?

I've been learning to paint. Badly, enthusiastically, with the kind of abandon I haven't felt since childhood. My teacher says I think too much, that I need to trust my instincts. But my instincts tell me to think, to analyze, to understand before I act. She says that's fear talking. Maybe she's right. Maybe all this thinking is just another way of avoiding the messy, uncertain business of living.

The coffee shop on Third Street closed last month. I hadn't been there in years, but knowing it's gone makes me sad in a way I didn't expect. We mourn the loss of possibilities, I think, more than the loss of actualities. I mourn all the conversations I might have had there, all the rainy afternoons I might have spent reading in the corner booth, all the versions of myself who might have become regulars.

There's a tree outside my window that's been there longer than this house. Sometimes I wonder what it's seen, what stories it could tell if trees kept histories. Does it remember the family who lived here before me? The ones before them? Or does it simply exist, moment to moment, season to season, without the burden of narrative that we humans carry?

I met a man at the library last week who collects first lines of books. Not the books themselves, just the first lines, written in careful handwriting in a leather journal. "It's all you need sometimes," he said. "The promise of a story." I've been thinking about that – about promises and beginnings and the faith required to believe that one sentence can lead somewhere worth going.

My mother used to say that the only constant is change, which seemed like another adult riddle when I was young. Now I see it's both true and incomplete. Yes, everything changes, but patterns persist. The spiral of a seashell echoes the spiral of galaxies. The branching of rivers mirrors the branching of blood vessels. There's a deep structure to things, a grammar of existence that remains even as the words change.

I've started walking without destinations. Not wandering, exactly – that implies aimlessness. It's more like following an invisible thread, turning left because left feels right, stopping when something catches my eye. Yesterday it led me to a small park I'd never noticed before, where an old man was teaching his granddaughter to skip stones. The look of concentration on her face was so fierce, so complete, that I stood watching longer than I should have.

The thing about words is that they're both precise and approximate. I can tell you the sky today is blue, but that tells you nothing about the particular quality of this blue – the way it deepens near the horizon, the way it seems to pulse with some internal light. Language is a bridge between minds, but it's a rickety bridge, full of gaps we fill with imagination and goodwill.

Time moves differently as we age. Not faster, exactly, but in larger chunks. Seasons blur together, years become difficult to distinguish. But moments – specific, crystalline moments – become more precious. The way light falls across the kitchen table at four in the afternoon. The sound of rain on different surfaces. The weight of a cat settling into your lap.

I think the lighthouse keeper was right. The sea does remember everything. And perhaps that's what we're all trying to do – to be like the sea, vast and deep and eternal, holding all our moments in solution, ready to wash them ashore when the tide is right.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman sat at the bus stop every Tuesday at 3:17 PM, even though no bus had serviced that route for eleven years. She wore the same lavender coat, carried the same patent leather purse, and held the same expired bus pass between her gloved fingers. I'd been watching her for three weeks now from the coffee shop across the street, and she never varied her routine.

Today, I decided to join her.

"Waiting for the 42?" I asked, settling onto the bench beside her.

She turned to me with eyes the color of winter sky. "Always."

The thing about abandoned bus stops is that they become repositories for lost things. Not just the obvious detritus—candy wrappers, forgotten umbrellas, metro cards—but the invisible accumulations. Conversations that never happened. Meetings that fell through. All the somewhere elses that people never quite reached.

"You know it doesn't run anymore," I said gently.

"Doesn't it?" She smiled, and I noticed her lipstick was the exact shade of the roses my grandmother used to grow. "I suppose that depends on your definition of 'run.'"

A pigeon landed near our feet, pecking at something that might have been bread or might have been concrete. In the city, sometimes it's hard to tell the difference between sustenance and structure.

"My daughter took the 42," she continued, smoothing her coat. "Every day to her job at the hospital. She was a nurse, you know. Pediatric ward. She had hands that could calm any frightened child."

I nodded, though something in her verb tense made my chest tighten.

"Twenty-three years she took this route. And then one day, she decided to walk instead. Said she needed the exercise, wanted to see the sunrise. Funny how one small decision can change everything."

The pigeon had been joined by others now, a congregation of grey and iridescent purple, their heads bobbing in some ancient rhythm. I wondered if they remembered when the buses ran, if they missed the reliable scatter of commuters dropping crumbs.

"Was that the day of the accident?" I asked, though I wasn't sure how I knew to ask it.

"Accident." She turned the word over like a smooth stone. "Such a tidy word for such an untidy thing. A drunk driver at 6:47 AM. Can you imagine? Who is drunk at 6:47 AM on a Wednesday?"

"I'm sorry."

"Yes," she said. "Everyone is always sorry. But sorry doesn't bring back the 42, does it?"

We sat in silence for a moment. The sun was starting its descent, painting the sky in shades of peach and gold that no painter could ever quite capture. This is the thing about beauty—it's always more profound when it's fleeting.

"You asked if I'm waiting for the 42," she said finally. "But that's not quite right. I'm not waiting for it. I'm remembering it. There's a difference."

"What's the difference?"

"Waiting implies hope. Remembering is just..." she gestured vaguely at the air, "...keeping company with what was."

A young couple walked past, absorbed in their phones, earbuds blocking out the world. They didn't notice us, didn't notice the empty bus stop, didn't notice the way the light was turning everything into gold leaf.

"My therapist says I need to 'move forward,'" the woman continued, making air quotes with her gloved hands. "But forward to what? My daughter is behind me. My memories are behind me. Everything I love is behind me. Why would I want to move away from that?"

I thought about my own ghosts then. The friend I'd lost to cancer last year. The relationship that ended not with a bang but with a slow, painful dissolve. All the people and moments that lived now only in the past tense.

"Maybe," I said slowly, "forward doesn't mean away. Maybe it just means carrying them differently."

She looked at me then, really looked at me, and I saw that her eyes weren't just the color of winter sky—they held winter's clarity too, that sharp, clean quality that comes after everything unnecessary has been stripped away.

"You're young," she said, but not unkindly. "You still think time is linear."

"Isn't it?"

"Only if you're not paying attention."

The shadows were lengthening now, reaching across the street like fingers. The coffee shop behind us was switching on its lights, preparing for the evening shift. Soon, the barista would start the ritual of cleaning the espresso machine, the hiss and gurgle marking another day's end.

"She would have been fifty-three next month," the woman said suddenly. "I was going to surprise her with a trip to Prague. She always wanted to see the astronomical clock. Said she wanted to stand there at noon and watch all those ancient figures come to life."

"You could still go."

"Could I?" She seemed to genuinely consider this. "Would it be the same, seeing it without her? Or would it be like those old photographs where someone's been cut out, and you can still see the outline of where they used to be?"

I didn't have an answer for that. Maybe there wasn't one.

The woman stood then, smoothing her coat once more, adjusting her purse on her shoulder. She moved with the careful precision of someone who knows that bones are more fragile than we pretend they are.

"Same time next week?" I asked.

She paused, turned back. "Will you be here?"

"I think so."

"Then perhaps I'll see you. Or perhaps I won't. Time isn't linear, remember?"

She walked away then, her lavender coat catching the last of the light. I sat on the bench a while longer, watching the pigeons disperse, feeling the weight of all the buses that would never come, all the daughters who would never return, all the Tuesdays that would arrive wearing the same face but holding different sorrows.

When I finally stood to leave, I noticed something on the bench where she'd been sitting. A single bus token, worn smooth by years of handling. I picked it up, turned it over in my palm. It was warm, as if it had been clutched tightly.

I pocketed it and walked back to the coffee shop. Tomorrow, I told myself, I would research when the 42 had stopped running. I would find out if there had been an accident. I would look for obituaries of pediatric nurses.

But tonight, I would just hold the token and think about time, about waiting, about the difference between moving forward and leaving behind. About the peculiar gravity of empty bus stops and the people who refuse to abandon them.

The barista nodded as I entered, already preparing my usual. Outside, the bench sat empty again, just another piece of urban furniture. But I knew better now. Some places are never really empty. They're just holding space for all the people who aren't there anymore, all the buses that no longer come, all the Tuesdays that accumulate like sediment in the heart.

Next week, I decided, I would be there at 3:17 PM.

Time might not be linear, but promises still are.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the bus stop wore mismatched gloves—one leather, one wool—and I couldn't stop staring. She caught me looking and smiled, revealing a gold tooth that caught the afternoon light. "They each have a story," she said, wiggling her fingers. The leather one, she explained, belonged to her late husband. The wool one she'd knitted herself during a particularly harsh winter when she couldn't afford to buy new ones.

I've been thinking about stories lately, how they cling to objects like barnacles to a ship's hull. My grandmother's rolling pin sits in my kitchen drawer, unused for years but too precious to discard. Sometimes I take it out just to feel the smooth wood worn down by decades of pie crusts and cookie dough. I can almost hear her humming while she worked, flour dusting her apron like snow.

Memory is a strange curator. It saves the oddest things while letting seemingly important moments slip through its fingers like water. I can't remember my college graduation speech, but I perfectly recall the taste of the lemonade at the corner store near my childhood home—too sweet, never cold enough, served in styrofoam cups that squeaked when you gripped them too tight.

The bus arrived twenty minutes late. The woman with mismatched gloves sat next to me, and we rode in comfortable silence through neighborhoods that seemed to be simultaneously decaying and gentrifying. A peculiar urban magic trick—artisanal coffee shops sprouting between check-cashing stores and beauty supplies shops with hand-painted signs. Progress, regression, or simply change? I couldn't decide.

She got off three stops before mine, patting my shoulder as she left. "Take care, dear," she said, and I knew I'd never see her again. That's the thing about cities—they're full of these brief intersections, lives touching for a moment before diverging forever. We're all just electrons bouncing off each other in some vast, incomprehensible experiment.

I've been reading about octopuses lately. They have three hearts and blue blood. Their arms can think independently. When threatened, some species can detach a limb and regrow it later. What would humans do with such abilities? We can barely handle one heart, let alone three. We love too much or too little, never quite finding the balance.

My apartment smells like rain even though it hasn't rained in weeks. The couple upstairs argues every Thursday at precisely 9:47 PM. Their voices rise and fall in familiar patterns, a weekly symphony of grievance and reconciliation. By 10:30, I hear laughter. By 11:00, silence. We all have our rituals.

Tomorrow marks five years since I moved to this city. Five years of learning its rhythms, its shortcuts, its secret places where you can still see stars despite the light pollution. There's a rooftop garden on 42nd Street that nobody seems to know about. I go there sometimes to think, surrounded by tomato plants and marigolds that some anonymous gardener tends with religious devotion.

I wonder about that gardener. Do they come at dawn? At dusk? Do they talk to the plants like I talk to my houseplants, confessing fears and dreams to leaves that listen without judgment? My philodendron knows more about my inner life than most of my friends.

The truth is, we're all just making it up as we go along. Every adult I knew as a child seemed to have it all figured out, but now I realize they were just better at pretending. We're all walking around with our mismatched gloves, trying to keep our hands warm in whatever way we can manage.

There's a particular shade of blue that only appears in the sky for about ten minutes after sunset. Not quite navy, not quite purple—a color that doesn't have a proper name. I've tried to photograph it dozens of times, but it never translates. Some things insist on being experienced rather than captured.

My phone buzzes. A friend asking if I want to grab dinner. I consider it, then remember I have leftover soup in the fridge, the kind that gets better with age. Sometimes solitude is a choice, not a sentence. Sometimes the best conversations are the ones we have with ourselves.

The streetlights are coming on now, one by one, like dominoes falling in reverse. Each pool of light reveals a small drama—a dog walker untangling leashes, teenagers sharing earbuds, a man reading a book at the bus stop, using his phone's flashlight to see the pages. We're all so desperate to continue our stories, to reach the next chapter before the light fades completely.

I think about the woman's mismatched gloves again. How perfect they were in their imperfection. How they told a story of loss and resilience, of making do and moving forward. Tomorrow, I might knit myself a single glove. Just to see what happens. Just to have half a story waiting for its other half to begin.

The city hums its evening song—sirens, laughter, music from open windows, the subway's distant rumble. It's a lullaby for insomniacs, a symphony for the sleepless. And somewhere in this vast ocean of concrete and dreams, we're all just trying to keep our hands warm, one mismatched glove at a time.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then, standing in his cramped kitchen that smelled of pipe tobacco and brine, watching him pour tea with hands that trembled like compass needles seeking north. But now, thirty years later, I find myself walking these same cliffs, and I think I understand what he meant.

Memory is a strange thing. It doesn't live in the brain alone—it lives in places, in the worn smooth stones beneath our feet, in the salt-heavy air that fills our lungs. The lighthouse is automated now, its keeper long buried in the hillside cemetery where wild roses grow between the headstones. But his words echo with each wave that crashes against the rocks below.

I came back here running from something, though I'd be hard-pressed to tell you what. A life that had grown too small, perhaps. A marriage that had slowly transformed from a garden into a museum—everything perfectly preserved but nothing alive. We'd stopped fighting years ago, which seemed like peace but was really just another kind of war, fought with silence instead of words.

The cottage I've rented sits a quarter-mile from the lighthouse, weather-beaten and honest in its simplicity. The windows rattle when the wind picks up, and there's a leak in the northwest corner that sings during storms. I've put a bucket there, and sometimes I lie awake listening to the rhythmic plink-plink-plink, a watery metronome marking time.

This morning, I found a seal pup on the beach. It looked at me with eyes like dark pools, unafraid, as if it knew something about me that I didn't know myself. We regarded each other for a long moment—two creatures temporarily sharing the same slice of universe—before it humped its way back to the water and disappeared beneath the green-grey surface.

I've been thinking about disappearance lately. How we can vanish even while remaining visible. How we can be sitting right across from someone at dinner and be as distant as stars. The last year of my marriage, I felt like a ghost haunting my own life, moving through rooms without disturbing the air.

But here, with the wind scraping my face raw and the gulls crying overhead, I feel solid again. Real. The cliffs don't care about my failed attempts at happiness. The sea doesn't judge my choices. They simply are, and in their presence, I can simply be.

I've started painting again. Nothing ambitious—just watercolors of the view from my window, trying to capture the way light moves across water, how it breaks and reforms and breaks again. My hands remember the weight of a brush, though it's been twenty years since I last held one. Back then, I painted because I thought I had something important to say. Now I paint because the light is beautiful and that's enough.

Yesterday, I met a woman in the village shop. She was buying candles and matches, preparing for a storm the weather service is predicting. She smiled at me—the kind of smile that starts in the eyes—and we talked about nothing in particular for ten minutes. Her name is Claire, and she runs the bakery on Harbor Street. She invited me for coffee, and I said yes, surprising myself with how easy the word felt in my mouth.

The storm arrived last night as promised, rattling the windows and making the cottage groan like a ship at sea. I sat by the fire, reading a book I'd found on the shelf—a collection of local legends, water-stained and missing its cover. One story caught my attention: a tale about a woman who threw her wedding ring into the sea and received in return the ability to understand the language of waves.

I don't wear my ring anymore. I left it on the kitchen counter the morning I left, a twenty-five-year circle of gold reflecting nothing. But I don't need magic to understand what the waves are saying. They speak of constant change, of how nothing stays the same, how even solid rock eventually yields to water's patient insistence.

The lighthouse keeper was right—the sea does remember everything. But unlike human memory, it doesn't hold on. Each wave that remembers also forgets, carries history up onto the shore and then retreats, leaving the sand smooth and ready for new stories to be written.

Tomorrow, I'm having coffee with Claire. Next week, I'll call my daughter and try to explain why I had to leave, why sometimes staying is the cruelest thing you can do. Next month, maybe I'll be ready to think about what comes after this intermission, this pause between acts.

But tonight, I'm sitting at my window, watching the automated lighthouse sweep its beam across dark water. The rain has stopped, and stars are beginning to show through the breaking clouds. Somewhere out there, a seal pup is learning to navigate currents. Somewhere, waves are remembering and forgetting, remembering and forgetting.

And I'm here, neither coming nor going, just being. For now, incredibly, that's enough.

The sea remembers everything, but it also knows when to let go.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the ocean remembers everything. Every ship that passed, every storm that raged, every quiet morning when the water lay still as glass. I didn't believe him then—I was young and thought memory lived only in minds. But now, standing here forty years later with salt spray on my face, I understand what he meant.

The rocks remember too. They hold the echoes of footsteps from generations of keepers climbing the spiral stairs. My grandfather's boots, my father's, mine. Soon, someone else's. The automation crew arrives tomorrow to install the LED beacon that will replace the Fresnel lens my great-grandfather polished every dawn for thirty years. Progress, they call it. More reliable than human hands.

I've spent the morning packing, though there isn't much to take. The brass telescope that watched for ships in distress. The logbooks dating back to 1892, each entry a small poem of weather and passing vessels. "Southeast wind, moderate chop. The Marianne passed at dawn, three lights burning bright." Thousands of such moments, recorded in fading ink.

My daughter called yesterday. "It's time, Dad. You can't live out there alone forever." She's right, of course. Seventy-three is too old to be hauling oil up those stairs, tending a light that machines can tend better. But she doesn't understand that I'm not alone here. The lighthouse is full of presence—not ghosts exactly, but the accumulated weight of all that watching, all that faithful tending.

In the kitchen, my coffee cup sits where I always place it, in the ring-stained spot on the wooden table. Through the window, I can see the path worn through the sea grass where I walk each evening to check the horizon. These small rituals have shaped my days for so long that I've forgotten who I was before them. Or perhaps I've become exactly who I was meant to be: a person who keeps watch, who maintains the light.

The beam still turns tonight, cutting through the darkness every ten seconds. I know its rhythm like my own heartbeat. Ships still navigate by it, though most have GPS now. Still, there's comfort in that sweeping light, that constant revolution. It says: someone is here, someone is watching, you are not alone in the dark.

Tomorrow they'll lock the door and hand me the key as a souvenir. They've promised to preserve the structure, turn it into a museum. Tourists will climb the stairs and read plaques about the history. They'll learn facts but miss the truth: that this place was alive, that it breathed with purpose, that it was the steady center around which the chaos of the sea arranged itself.

I think about the storms I've weathered here. The hurricane of '98 when the windows blew out and I had to crawl up the stairs on my belly to keep the light burning. The winter when ice covered everything and I chipped it away with a hammer, hour after hour, so the beam could shine. The night I guided in the fishing boat with the failed engine, watching through the telescope as they fought the waves, adjusting the foghorn pattern so they could follow it home.

These memories layer like sediment. This is what the old keeper meant—the ocean remembers because we remember, because generation after generation has stood here witnessing, recording, participating in the endless conversation between land and sea.

The automation makes sense. I know this. The LED will burn for years without maintenance. It won't get tired or old. It won't mistake a star for a ship's light on a clear night. It won't worry about the sailor's wives waiting on shore or wonder about the cargo in a ship's hold. It will simply burn, precise and constant.

But it won't know the stories. It won't recognize the fishing boats by their running lights or notice when someone's heading out in weather they shouldn't. It won't understand the language of the waves against the rocks, warning of storms still beyond the horizon. All that knowledge, accumulated over centuries, will scatter like foam on the wind.

I've saved one logbook to take with me—my father's from his first year as keeper. His handwriting was terrible, but his observations were poetry. "Fog thick as wool. The horn speaks to nothing but receives echoes from everywhere." Reading it, I can see him young again, overwhelmed by the responsibility, slowly learning the lighthouse's rhythms.

The sun sets now, painting the sky colors that have no names. I make my final entry in the log: "Clear evening, light wind from the northwest. All vessels safely passed. Light burning bright." Then I close the book and climb the stairs one last time.

At the top, I stand beside the great lens, watching it turn. Each prism catches the light and throws it seaward, a million tiny rainbows spinning through the dark. Tomorrow this will end, but tonight the light still turns, still reaches across the water, still says what it has always said: Here is safety. Here is home. Someone is watching.

The ocean remembers everything. And so do I.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't understand what he meant until I inherited his job and spent my first winter alone on this rocky outcrop, listening to the waves crash against the foundation stones that have been here since 1847.

There's a rhythm to solitude that city people never quite grasp. It's not empty—it's full of small ceremonies. The morning inspection of the lens. The afternoon weather log. The evening ritual of winding the clockwork mechanism that turns the light. These routines become a kind of prayer, a devotion to the idea that somewhere out there in the darkness, someone might need this beam to find their way home.

I've started collecting things the tide brings in. Smooth glass bottles with no messages inside. Driftwood twisted into impossible shapes. Once, a child's piano, barnacle-crusted and missing half its keys. I keep them in the storage room beneath the tower, my museum of the lost and found. Sometimes I imagine the stories behind them—the ship's captain who threw that bottle overboard in frustration, the family whose beach house surrendered that piano to a hurricane.

The lighthouse itself speaks. Not in words, but in groans and sighs as the temperature changes, in the whistle of wind through the gallery rails, in the percussion of rain on the lamp room's bronze roof. After six months, I could diagnose its moods from my bed. That particular creak means the northwest wind is picking up. That settling sound means the foundation is adjusting to the low tide.

There are exactly 217 steps from the bottom to the top. I know because I count them every time, and the number never changes, though some nights it feels like a thousand. The spiral staircase is painted white, but in certain lights—especially during the golden hour before sunset—it turns the color of old bones.

I've seen things from the gallery that no one would believe. Whales breaching in perfect synchronization under the full moon. St. Elmo's fire dancing along the railing during a storm. Once, a flock of migrating birds, exhausted and disoriented, landed all around me. I sat perfectly still for hours as they rested, feeling like I'd been trusted with something sacred.

The supply boat comes once a month. The captain, a taciturn woman named Chen, brings groceries, fuel for the backup generator, and occasional letters from my sister asking when I'm going to return to civilization. I never know how to answer her. How do you explain that civilization feels less real than this? That I've never felt more present than I do watching the sun set from my circular horizon?

There's a logbook in the lamp room dating back to the 1920s. Previous keepers recorded not just weather and ships sighted, but personal observations. "Margaret's birthday today—lit an extra flare in celebration." "Saw the green flash at sunset. First time in forty years." "The cormorants have returned early. What do they know?" I've started adding my own entries, wondering who might read them decades from now.

The toughest part isn't the isolation—it's the responsibility. Knowing that the light must not fail. Even with modern GPS and radar, boats still rely on lighthouses. We're the last analog backup in an increasingly digital world. During the worst storms, when the power flickers and the backup generator roars to life, I stand in the lamp room and watch the beam cut through the chaos, steady as a heartbeat.

I've learned to cook with whatever Chen brings, supplemented by the small garden I've coaxed to life in the lee of the tower. Tomatoes that taste like the sea. Herbs that grow wild and strong. I dry them in the lamp room, where the concentrated sun turns them into concentrated flavor.

Sometimes tourists boat close enough to take pictures. I wave from the gallery, playing the part of the quaint lighthouse keeper. They don't see the 3 AM maintenance checks, the battle against rust and erosion, the careful mathematics of fuel consumption versus storm predictions. They see a postcard, not a life.

But it is a life. Fuller than the one I left behind in the city, where I was always between things—between meetings, between appointments, between the person I was and the person I was supposed to become. Here, I am simply the keeper of the light. The job is clear. The purpose is evident. The horizon is infinite.

Last week, during a particularly fierce storm, I spotted a small sailboat struggling against the waves. I watched through the telescope as they fought to find the harbor entrance, using my light as their guide. When they finally made it to safety, they flashed their lights three times—a thank you in the universal language of the sea.

That's when I understood what the old keeper meant. The sea remembers everything—every storm weathered, every life saved, every lonely night when the light pierced the darkness. And now I'm part of that memory, another keeper in an unbroken chain stretching back through time, tending a flame that must not go out.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman's hands moved with practiced precision, folding the letter along creases that had become soft from repetition. She'd read it so many times that the words had worn grooves in her memory, each phrase a familiar path her mind could travel with eyes closed.

*Dear Eleanor,* it began, in handwriting that had once been crisp but now appeared to shimmer and fade like morning mist.

Outside her window, the city had changed. Where once stood the corner grocery with its red awning and Mr. Chen's cheerful whistle, there was now a glass tower that caught the sunset and threw it back in fragments. Progress, they called it. She called it forgetting.

But she remembered.

She remembered the taste of penny candy melting on her tongue, the weight of her mother's hand on her shoulder, the way the floorboards in their old apartment creaked a symphony when she danced alone in the living room. She remembered Timothy, who wrote this letter, standing at the train station with his hat tilted just so, trying to look brave.

The letter spoke of ordinary things – the view from his barracks, the quality of the food, a joke someone had told at dinner. He'd been careful not to worry her. Between the lines, though, she could read the spaces where fear lived, the careful omissions that said more than words ever could.

Seventy years. That's how long she'd been reading this letter, the last one that arrived before the telegram that changed everything.

Eleanor stood, her movements deliberate despite the protests of ancient joints. In the kitchen, she put the kettle on – the same ritual, the same brown teapot with the chip on the handle that she'd never had the heart to replace. Some things deserved to persist.

The water boiled, singing its familiar song. She poured it over the leaves and watched them unfold like tiny hands opening to prayer. While it steeped, she returned to her chair and picked up her pen.

*Dear Timothy,* she wrote, as she did every week.

She told him about the city's changes, about Mrs. Patterson's granddaughter who'd just had twins, about the new coffee shop where they played jazz on Thursday nights. She wrote about the cardinal that visited her fire escape each morning, bold and red against the grey concrete. She wrote about the books she'd read, the way the light fell differently now that they'd cut down the oak across the street.

She never mailed these letters. They accumulated in shoeboxes beneath her bed, a one-sided conversation spanning decades. But the writing itself was the point – a bridge across time, a defiance of absence.

The doorbell rang. Eleanor glanced at the clock; Meals on Wheels was early today. She tucked both letters into the drawer and made her way to the door.

"Hi, Mrs. Chen!" The volunteer was young, perhaps twenty-five, with energy that seemed to bubble from her very pores. "I brought your favorite – chicken and dumplings."

"Thank you, dear." Eleanor accepted the container, still warm. "Would you like some tea?"

The girl – Samantha, Eleanor remembered – hesitated. "I shouldn't. I have six more deliveries."

"Just a quick cup," Eleanor insisted. There was something in the girl's eyes today, a shadow that hadn't been there last week.

They sat together, the steam rising between them like incense. Samantha's hands wrapped around the delicate china cup as if drawing strength from its warmth.

"My boyfriend deployed last week," she said suddenly. "Afghanistan."

Eleanor nodded, understanding flooding through her. "Tell me about him."

And Samantha did – about his laugh, his terrible cooking, the way he sang in the shower. Eleanor listened, really listened, the way people rarely did anymore. When Samantha left, her step was lighter, the shadow lifted if not erased.

Alone again, Eleanor returned to her letter. She had more to tell Timothy now – about Samantha, about the way love persists across distance and time, about how some things never really change despite all appearances.

The sun was setting, painting the glass towers in shades of rose and gold. Beautiful, in its way. Different, but beautiful.

She thought about Timothy's hands, how they'd felt holding hers. She thought about all the letters she'd written, all the words she'd sent into the silence. Were they prayers? Promises? Perhaps just proof that she'd continued, that their story hadn't ended with a telegram and a folded flag.

Tomorrow she would write again. Tomorrow she would tell him about the dream she'd had where they danced in their old living room, the floorboards singing beneath their feet. She would tell him about the taste of penny candy she'd found at a vintage shop downtown, how it had transported her across seven decades in an instant.

But tonight, she would simply sit with his letter in her lap, watching the city lights bloom like earthbound stars, remembering and remembering and remembering.

Because someone should. Someone must.

And love, real love, is just another word for refusing to forget.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me something once that I've never forgotten: "The ocean doesn't care about your plans." 

I was twenty-three and thought I knew everything about everything. I'd driven up the coast to photograph lighthouses for a magazine piece that would never get published. The keeper, Marcus, was supposed to be just another interview, another quirky local to add color to my story. Instead, he became the person who taught me how to see.

"You're trying too hard," he said, watching me fumble with my camera settings as the sun began its descent. "The best light isn't something you capture. It's something you witness."

I wanted to argue, to explain about golden hour and optimal exposure, but something in his weathered face stopped me. He'd been keeping this lighthouse for forty years. Every sunset, every storm, every clear morning when the dolphins played in the surf—he'd seen them all from this same spot.

"Come on," he said, leading me up the spiral stairs. "I'll show you something."

The lighthouse lens room was smaller than I expected, filled with the smell of metal polish and old leather. The Fresnel lens dominated the space, its prismatic surface catching the dying light and throwing rainbows across the walls. Marcus moved around it with practiced ease, checking mechanisms I couldn't name.

"People think lighthouse keeping is about the light," he said, running his hand along the brass fittings. "But really, it's about the darkness. The light's just there to push it back a little."

I thought he was being poetic until later that night, when the fog rolled in. I'd never seen anything like it—a living wall of gray that swallowed the world. From the ground, it was disorienting, almost frightening. But from the top of the lighthouse, watching our beam cut through it in steady revolutions, I understood. The darkness wasn't the enemy. It was just the canvas.

Marcus made coffee on a camping stove, the blue flame the only light besides the beacon above us. We sat in folding chairs on the gallery, wrapped in wool blankets that smelled like salt and time.

"My daughter lives in Phoenix now," he said suddenly. "Keeps asking when I'm going to retire, move somewhere sensible. She doesn't understand that this is my sensible. You can't explain the ocean to someone who lives in the desert."

A ship's horn echoed through the fog, low and mournful. Marcus counted under his breath—one Mississippi, two Mississippi—then smiled. "That's the Cassandra. Cargo vessel out of Seattle. Captain always sounds his horn at the same spot, like he's saying hello."

"How can you tell?"

"Same way you can tell your friend's laugh in a crowded room. When you pay attention long enough, everything has its own voice."

We sat in comfortable silence, watching the beam sweep through the nothingness. Sometimes a gull would appear in the light, ghost-white and startled, before vanishing again. I found myself thinking about all the nights Marcus had spent here, all the ships he'd guided past the rocks, all the storms he'd weathered.

"Were you ever scared?" I asked. "During the bad storms?"

He considered this, sipping his coffee. "Fear and respect aren't the same thing, but they're cousins. The ocean's bigger than any of us. Older. It was here before the first lighthouse, and it'll be here after the last one goes dark. But that doesn't mean we shouldn't light them."

Around three in the morning, the fog began to thin. Stars appeared, first one by one, then in crowds. The Milky Way emerged like a river of light across the sky. I'd never seen it so clearly.

"That's the other thing about darkness," Marcus said. "Without it, you'd never see the stars."

I stayed for three more days, ostensibly working on my article but really just listening to Marcus's stories. He told me about the whale that got tangled in fishing nets and how he'd called the Coast Guard to save it. About the couple who got married on the gallery during a meteor shower. About the night the light mechanism failed and he had to hand-crank it for six hours until help arrived.

On my last morning, he made eggs on the camping stove and we watched the sunrise paint the ocean gold and pink and impossible blue. The dolphins were there, just as he'd promised, their bodies dark semicolons punctuating the swells.

"You'll be back," he said as I packed my camera gear.

"How do you know?"

He smiled, the kind of smile that comes from knowing things you can't explain. "Because now you've seen it. Really seen it. And once you've seen something true, you can't unsee it."

He was right, of course. I did go back, many times over the years. Sometimes with cameras, sometimes without. Marcus retired eventually—his daughter finally won that battle—but he trained his replacement well. The light still turns, still pushes back the darkness, still guides ships home.

I think about him often, especially on foggy nights when the world feels too big and too close all at once. I think about what he taught me: that seeing isn't about capturing or owning or even understanding. Sometimes it's just about being present when the light meets the dark, and knowing that both are necessary.

The ocean still doesn't care about our plans. But maybe that's the point. Maybe the best we can do is tend our lights, watch for ships in the fog, and pay attention to the voices in the darkness. Maybe that's enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman's hands moved across the piano keys like water finding its way downhill—not rushed, but inevitable. She played in the empty concert hall every Tuesday at 3 AM, when the security guard took his break and the ghost lights cast long shadows between the rows of red velvet seats.

Nobody knew her name. The cleaning staff called her "the moth" because of her gray coat and the way she seemed drawn to the single bulb left burning on stage. She never spoke, just nodded when she arrived and again when she left, always through the stage door that someone had forgotten to fix twenty years ago and now refused to lock properly.

The music she played wasn't written anywhere. She had tried to explain once, years ago, that the notes lived in the wood of the piano, in the stretched steel of the strings, in the felt hammers worn smooth by a century of concerts. All she did was let them out.

On this particular Tuesday, something was different. A child sat in the back row—a girl, maybe eight, with dark hair in two uneven braids. She wasn't supposed to be there. Nobody was supposed to be there. But the old woman continued playing, and the child continued listening, and the empty hall held them both like a cupped palm.

The music that night was about rain on a tin roof, about the smell of bread baking at four in the morning, about the way light breaks through cathedral windows. It was about things that have no names in any language but that everyone recognizes when they feel them.

When the last note faded, the child stood up. She walked down the aisle—not running, not hesitant, just walking like she knew exactly where she was going. She climbed the steps to the stage and sat beside the old woman on the piano bench.

"I hear them too," the child said. "The notes in the wood."

The old woman's hands, which had been so sure on the keys, trembled as she reached into her coat pocket. She pulled out a small brass key, worn smooth by decades of handling. She had carried it every day since she was eight years old, when another gray-coated figure had pressed it into her palm in this very hall.

"Then it's yours now," she said, her voice rusty with disuse. "Every Tuesday. Three o'clock. The guard takes his break for exactly forty-five minutes."

The child took the key, weighing it in her small hand. "What about you?"

"I'll be in the wood," the old woman said. "In the strings. In the felt. Waiting to come out when you play."

The child nodded. This made perfect sense to her in the way that true things do to children before the world teaches them to doubt.

The old woman stood, her gray coat swirling around her like wings. She walked to the stage door, then paused. Without turning, she said, "The piano's name is Rosalie. She likes Chopin on rainy nights and Bach when the moon is full. But mostly she likes the songs that don't have names yet."

Then she was gone, leaving only the scent of lavender and old sheet music.

The child sat alone on the bench, her feet dangling above the pedals. She placed her hands on the keys, feeling the warmth the old woman's fingers had left behind. Slowly, carefully, she pressed middle C.

The note rang out in the empty hall, rich and full and somehow larger than the space that contained it. It sounded like a greeting. It sounded like a promise. It sounded like the first word of a conversation that would last a lifetime.

Outside, the city slept on, unaware that in a forgotten concert hall, the music had found its new guardian. The child played until the security guard returned, then slipped out through the broken stage door, the brass key warm in her pocket.

The next Tuesday, she would return. And the Tuesday after that. And all the Tuesdays stretching forward like an unplayed melody, waiting to be discovered.

In the rafters above the stage, a moth circled the ghost light. If anyone had been there to see, they might have noticed that its wings, catching the light, looked almost like gray wool. Almost like the hem of an old coat.

But no one was there to see. There was only the piano, patient as always, its wood full of songs, its strings vibrating with possibilities, waiting for small hands to set them free.

Somewhere in the city, an old woman's apartment sat empty, her bed unslept in, her tea cold on the kitchen table. But if you pressed your ear to the wall, you could hear music—faint but unmistakable, like a memory of a song you learned before you had words for singing.

The piano played on, Tuesday after Tuesday, century after century. The players changed, but the music remained—not written, not taught, but passed down in brass keys and patient wood, in the space between silence and sound where all true songs are born.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the ocean remembers everything. Every ship that's passed, every storm that's raged, every secret dropped into its depths. I didn't believe him then, but now, standing at the edge of the pier with salt spray stinging my face, I wonder if he was right.

My grandmother's letters arrived yesterday—a bundle tied with kitchen twine, discovered in the walls during renovation. Sixty-three letters, never sent, all addressed to someone named Catherine. My grandmother's name was Rose.

"My dearest C," the first one begins, "The boats came back without him today."

I've been reading them in order, these messages that were meant to travel across an ocean but instead traveled through time. They tell a story I never knew, about a woman I thought I understood. Rose, who made blackberry jam every August and sang hymns while washing dishes. Rose, who apparently loved someone named Catherine with a fierceness that practically burns through the aged paper.

The lighthouse beam sweeps across the water, a metronome keeping time. One rotation every ten seconds. I've counted. It's something to do while thinking about how many times we believe we know someone.

In letter number thirty-seven, Rose writes about a plan to leave. "After the war," she promises. "After James comes home and I can hand him the keys to the store. After Mother passes. After, after, after." The word appears seventeen times in that one letter. I counted that too.

But there was no after. James didn't come home. Mother lived another forty years. And Rose married my grandfather, a kind man who sold insurance and never raised his voice. They had my mother, then my aunt, then my uncle. A proper family. A proper life.

Catherine appears in the town records exactly twice: once in 1943, when she bought the old Garrison place, and once in 1947, when she sold it. Four years. That's all. Four years in a place where families stretch back centuries, where everyone knows everyone, where secrets are currency.

"Do you remember a Catherine?" I asked Mrs. Chen at the post office. She's ninety-four and sharp as winter wind.

"Catherine Garrison? Course I do. Kept to herself. City woman. Had the strangest accent—not quite British, not quite anything." She paused, sorting mail with practiced efficiency. "Why?"

I couldn't say. Couldn't betray Rose's secret after she'd kept it so well. "Just curious about the old houses," I lied.

Mrs. Chen gave me a look that said she knew I was lying but wouldn't press. Small-town courtesy.

The letters grow desperate as they progress. Rose's handwriting, usually so careful, becomes rushed. Ink blots mar the pages where her pen pressed too hard. "C, are you getting these? Are you reading them? The silence is killing me."

But of course Catherine wasn't getting them. They were all here, in the walls, insulation for a love that couldn't be spoken aloud.

I think about my own secrets, smaller and more ordinary. The manuscript I've hidden in my desk drawer. The plane ticket I bought and never used. The phone calls I don't return. We all have walls, I suppose. Some of us just hide our letters in them.

The lighthouse beam continues its circuit. Patient. Constant. Warning ships away from the rocks.

In the last letter, dated October 12, 1947, Rose writes: "I saw you at the station today. You wore the blue coat I always loved. You looked directly at me, and I couldn't move, couldn't speak, couldn't breathe. Then you boarded the train. I know you won't come back. I know this is goodbye. I know I'm the one who chose this. God help me, I'm the one who chose this."

No more letters after that. Just fifty years of marriage, three children, seven grandchildren, and a life that looked perfect from the outside. Church on Sundays. Rotary Club on Wednesdays. Bridge on Fridays. A rhythm as steady as the lighthouse beam.

I fold the letters carefully, return them to their bundle. Tomorrow I'll decide what to do with them. Whether to keep Rose's secret or set it free. Whether it matters now, when everyone involved is gone.

The ocean holds so much—ships and storms and secrets. Bodies and bottles and broken promises. Love letters that were never sent, and some that were. All of it dissolved in salt water, transformed into something else. Memory, maybe. Or just the endless whisper of waves against the shore.

I wonder if Catherine kept letters too. If somewhere, in another wall or attic or safe deposit box, Rose's name waits to be discovered. If their story has another side, another perspective, another kind of truth.

The lighthouse beam sweeps over me, and for a moment I'm illuminated, then cast back into darkness. This is how it feels, I think, to discover that the people we love were whole human beings before we knew them, and remained whole human beings despite how we knew them.

My grandmother Rose. Who made blackberry jam and sang hymns and loved someone named Catherine with a fierceness that burned through paper, through time, through the careful construction of a proper life.

The ocean remembers everything, the lighthouse keeper said.

Maybe that's enough. Maybe that's all we can ask for—that somewhere, somehow, the love we couldn't speak aloud is still held, still remembered, still turning with the tides.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then, standing on those weathered rocks with saltwater misting my face, but now I wonder if he knew something I didn't. Maybe that's why I keep coming back to this shore, drawn like driftwood to the tideline.

I've been thinking about memory lately—how it ebbs and flows, how certain moments crystallize while others dissolve like seafoam. My grandmother used to make these intricate paper cranes, hundreds of them, filling mason jars throughout her house. She said each one held a wish. After she died, we found thousands, some so old the paper had yellowed and grown soft as cloth. We never knew what she wished for.

There's a coffee shop in Portland where the barista knows my name but always spells it wrong on the cup. I've never corrected her. Sometimes the small mysteries are worth preserving. Like how my neighbor leaves tiny painted rocks on people's doorsteps—I've collected seven so far, each with a different constellation. Or how the library downtown still has that same musty smell from when I was eight, hiding between the stacks reading books about kids who solved mysteries and saved the day.

I tried to save a bird once. Found it on the sidewalk, wing bent at an impossible angle. Carried it home in my cupped palms, made a nest from shredded newspaper in a shoebox. My mother helped me feed it water from an eyedropper. It died in the night, and I learned that wanting something badly enough doesn't make it true. But I also learned that trying matters, that the attempt itself is a kind of prayer.

The world feels heavier now than it used to. Or maybe I'm just more aware of the weight. News cycles spin faster than weather systems, and somewhere between the headlines and the hashtags, I lose track of what's real. My friend texts me photos of her garden—tomatoes growing despite the heat, sunflowers turning their faces east. These small acts of cultivation feel like rebellion, like hope.

I've started walking again. Early mornings when the streets are empty except for dog walkers and delivery trucks. There's a man who sits on the same bench every day feeding pigeons. We nod at each other, companions in this ritual of dawn. The birds know him, cluster around his feet in anticipation. I wonder if they mourn him on the days he doesn't come.

My father taught me to skip stones when I was seven. The secret, he said, is in the wrist—a snap at just the right moment. We'd spend hours at the lake, searching for perfect flat stones, competing to see who could get the most skips. Twenty years later, I taught my niece the same technique. She got five skips on her third try, and her joy was so pure it made my chest ache.
